The lesson is to check what your opponent is threatening before you make a natural-looking capture: here e5 was less about the pawn and more of a trap on your centralized knight.

Takeaway: Before capturing, ask what your opponent gains in return — sometimes a "free" pawn costs you your best piece.

I'd play Bb4. Your fxe5 is understandable because it removes the White pawn on e5, but Bb4 is more active: it attacks the rook on e1 while also defending your knight on a5. That means you improve a loose piece and make White respond to your move, which is a very reusable way to handle positions with undefended pieces.  
Takeaway: When you can make a forcing move that also fixes one of your loose pieces, look at that before making a simple capture.

I'd play O-O-O.

Taking on e5 is a very natural reaction to eliminate the pawn attacking your bishop, but it leaves your knight on d5 undefended and vulnerable to White's queen. Instead, castling queenside is a fantastic way to handle the tension while improving your position. If White captures your bishop, you simply recapture with your rook, which brings your king to safety and activates your rook on a powerful central file. 

Takeaway: When dealing with a threat, look for active ways to ignore or sidestep it that simultaneously develop your pieces and keep your king safe.

I'd play Qd7. Instead of shifting the rook to e8, this move improves your queen with a clear purpose: it attacks h3 while also defending your knight on c6 and bishop on c7. A simple plan is Qd7, and if White plays Nh4, Ba5 keeps pressure by attacking the knight on c3.  
Takeaway: Prefer multi-purpose moves that create pressure while strengthening your own pieces.

I'd play Ba5. Your Rfe8 was a natural developing move, but it didn't create any pressure — it just shuffled a rook to a file where nothing is happening yet. Ba5 puts a real question to the knight on c3, which is one of White's key defenders of the center; if that knight moves or you can trade it off, White's grip on d5 and e4 loosens and your pieces get more active. The follow-up is simple: keep an eye on the center and reroute your queen to d7 to stay flexible while the pin does its work.

Takeaway: Before a routine developing move, ask whether you can instead make a piece attack something — active moves that pose problems usually beat quiet ones.

I'd play Qd7. 

Your move Rfe8 allows White to improve their position with a free tempo by playing Bf4 to attack your queen. Stepping back to d7 anticipates this natural developing move, gets your queen to a safe square, and keeps your pieces well-coordinated. By playing prophylactically, you dictate the pace of the game rather than letting White push your pieces around.

Takeaway: Anticipating your opponent's natural developing moves and moving your major pieces out of their crosshairs saves you crucial tempos.

I'd play Be6. Your Bf5 develops, but Be6 is more purposeful: it develops while attacking the bishop on g8 and defending the pawn on d5. That means Black improves a piece and fixes a loose-pawn problem in one move, instead of only defending the queen on e4. The plan is simple: make White answer the attack, then keep developing.

Takeaway: Look for developing moves that also solve a concrete problem.

I'd play Be6. Your bishop move was the right instinct, but Bf5 left your weak d5-pawn hanging while offering the enemy an annoying check first. Instead, Be6 develops that same bishop while doing two jobs at once: it directly challenges the strong bishop sitting on g8, and it firmly defends your pawn on d5. If that bishop takes yours, your queen happily recaptures, and you've traded off an attacker and steadied your center.

Takeaway: When you develop a piece, try to pick the square where it also defends your weak spots.

I'd play Ra4. The check with Ra3+ is tempting, but here the more instructive plan is to improve the rook with a clear target: Ra4 attacks the pawn on b4. If White does not solve that problem, Black can follow with Rxb4, so you are creating pressure instead of just giving a check.  
Takeaway: In rook endings, prefer active moves that attack pawns over checks that do not win anything concrete.

I'd play Be6. 

Your move Bf5 is a perfectly normal way to develop your bishop, but in this position, you have a chance to solve multiple problems with a single move. By playing Be6, you bring your last minor piece into the game while simultaneously defending your attacked d5 pawn from the White queen. It also directly challenges White's awkwardly placed bishop on g8, and if White decides to trade bishops, you can comfortably recapture with your queen. 

Takeaway: When deciding how to develop a piece, look for the square that lets it do double duty, like defending a weak pawn or challenging an enemy piece.

I'd play Ra4. Your Ra3+ feels natural, but a check that just pushes the enemy king toward the center (where it wants to go to support those passed pawns) rarely helps — it's a "move with tempo" that actually improves your opponent's position. Instead, grab something real: Ra4 hits the b4-pawn and, after White defends with something like Rf6, you can snap it off with Rxb4, winning a pawn and starting to chew into White's queenside majority. In this kind of position your rook should be doing active, concrete work against pawns rather than giving loose checks.

Takeaway: Don't check just because you can — a check that drives the king somewhere useful is a gift to your opponent, so prefer moves that win material or targets.

I'd play Ra4. 

While giving a check with Ra3+ is very tempting, it actually helps White by pushing their king to e4, where it becomes much more active and central. Instead, playing Ra4 directly attacks the undefended b4 pawn. Since White's rook is tied down or out of position to defend it properly, you will win that pawn and create a passed pawn of your own on the queenside, making your winning task much simpler.

Takeaway: In endgames, avoid giving checks that only help your opponent improve their king position; focus on winning material and creating passed pawns instead.

I'd play Bh6. Instead of grabbing that pawn on d4, look for a move that develops a piece AND creates a threat — here your bishop springs out and attacks the enemy queen, forcing it to move where you want. Trading pawns with exd4 just invited the queen to a strong central square for free; with Bh6 you gain time by making your opponent react while you get a piece into the game. Get your pieces active and put your opponent on the defensive.

Takeaway: A developing move that also makes a threat is worth far more than winning a single pawn.

I'd play Qa5+. This is a great beginner move because it starts with a check, so White must respond, and it also protects your loose e5 pawn. Compared with exd4, you are not just trading in the center right away; you improve your queen to an active square with tempo and keep useful pressure. After that, your plan is to keep developing your pieces while White is busy answering your threats.

Takeaway: When you can make a forcing move that also defends a loose pawn, look at it before making a simple capture.

I'd play Rd8. Your Rc8 attacked the bishop on c4, but it let White choose how to respond while the pawn on d6 stayed strong. Rd8 is more direct: put the rook in front of the passed pawn and make White prove it can advance safely. A sample idea is Rd8 Bxe6+ Kh8, and Black has kept attention on White’s dangerous pawn instead of just chasing a bishop.

Takeaway: When the opponent has an advanced passed pawn, first look for the move that blockades or attacks it directly.

I'd play Bh6. 

By capturing with exd4, you released the tension in the center and allowed White's queen to comfortably recapture on d4. Instead, playing Bh6 develops a piece while directly attacking White's exposed queen. This forces White to react to your threat, gaining you a "free" turn (a tempo) to continue developing your pieces and getting your king to safety.

Takeaway: Whenever possible, look for developing moves that also create an immediate threat, especially against an exposed enemy queen.

I'd play b5. Your Rc8 had the right idea of hitting the bishop on c4, but a pawn does the same job while keeping your rook free for more important work. After b5 you kick the bishop and gain some space on the queenside, and even if White grabs the pawn on e6 with check, you simply tuck your king to h8 and keep playing. The key difference from Rc8 is that here your rook stays active and ready to deal with that dangerous d6 pawn instead of drifting to the side.

Takeaway: When you can chase a piece with a pawn instead of a piece, do it — it saves your stronger pieces for bigger tasks.

I'd play Qd4+. This is a forcing move: it gives check while also defending your rook on d1 and your pawn on d7. Compared with Qe8, it keeps your queen active and makes Black respond first, so your next plan can be to stay coordinated, for example after ...f6 with Qd3.  
Takeaway: When under pressure, look for forcing queen moves that also defend your key pieces.

I'd play b5. 

You're in a very tough spot down significant material, so your goal is to create as many practical problems for White as possible. While your move Rc8 attacks the bishop on c4, pushing b5 immediately kicks that bishop and forces it to make a decision right away. This disrupts White's coordination and ensures they have to keep calculating, rather than giving them a free hand to execute their plans.

Takeaway: When defending a difficult endgame, actively challenge your opponent's best pieces to put up the maximum possible resistance.

I'd play Qd4+. Your move Qe8 sent the queen to a square where it did nothing useful and left your king exposed — the queen belongs in the center where it does several jobs at once. From d4 it checks the king, guards your rook on d1, protects your advanced d-pawn, and eyes the b2-pawn all in one move, which is exactly the kind of active, multitasking placement you want when defending a tricky position. After the check, tuck things together and keep pushing that dangerous d-pawn as your trump card.

Takeaway: When under pressure, look for one active move that defends and threatens at the same time instead of drifting your queen to the edge.

I'd play Qd4+. 

Your move, Qe8, unfortunately just gives away the queen to one of Black's rooks. When you are relying on advanced passed pawns like the one on d7, you have to keep your heavy pieces coordinated to support them. By playing Qd4+, you centralize your queen with tempo by checking the king, while simultaneously defending your crucial d7 pawn and eyeing Black's dangerous b2 pawn. 

Takeaway: Centralizing your queen with check is often a highly effective way to stabilize your position and support your advanced pawns.

I'd play Rf7. Instead of chasing the queen with Rf5, this calmly improves the rook while defending both b7 and g7. A simple plan is to meet c6 with bxc6, keeping your position solid and your extra activity under control.  
Takeaway: When you are already doing well, prefer a consolidating move that protects key pawns before starting new attacks.

I'd play Rf7. Your Rf5 was tempting because it hits the queen, but attacking a piece isn't useful if the square isn't safe — the queen simply steps aside and your rook ends up loose and offside. The real problem in your position is that your rook on f6 is exposed and your queenside pawns need attention; Rf7 tucks the rook onto a safe square while defending both b7 and g7, keeping your position solid so you can meet White's break with c6 calmly.

Takeaway: Before you chase an enemy piece, make sure your own attacking piece lands on a safe square and actually improves your position.

I'd play Rgd1. Your Ne7+ rushes the loose knight forward, but the important first job is to make your position stable. Rgd1 does that by defending both the rook on c1 and the knight on d5, so your active pieces stay coordinated instead of becoming targets. Once your pieces are protected, you can look for active moves with much more confidence.

Takeaway: Before forcing moves, check whether your loose pieces need support.

I'd play Rf7.

Your rook on f6 was under attack by White's g5 pawn, so moving it was absolutely the right instinct. While attacking the queen with your move seems active, it allows White's queen to slide to d6 where it becomes a real nuisance in your position. Dropping the rook back to f7 safely removes it from danger while beautifully defending your pawns on b7 and g7 against any future threats.

Takeaway: When an attacked piece has to retreat, look for a square that solidifies your defense rather than a move that forces your opponent's pieces to more active squares.

I'd play Rgd1. The check with Ne7+ feels tempting, but forcing moves aren't automatically good — after the king steps aside your knight is stranded and hanging, and you'd lose it for nothing. Instead, notice that your knight on d5 is undefended right now, and Rgd1 quietly supports it while also backing up your rook on c1; once your pieces are solid you can push b6 to open lines against the black king. Fix the loose piece first, then attack.

Takeaway: Before you throw in a check, make sure your own pieces are defended — a threat that loses material isn't really a threat.

I'd play Ngf6. This develops a minor piece and adds support to your d5 pawn and your knight on d7, so it improves your position without creating new problems. Compared with Bc5, this is a cleaner beginner habit: bring a piece out to a useful square first, then continue with simple development such as Bg7.

I'd play Rgd1. 

Giving a check with Ne7+ is very tempting, but it abandons your knight's fantastic central outpost on d5. When you have a beautifully placed, aggressive piece like that knight, you want to keep it there! By playing Rgd1, you firmly defend the knight and maintain your strong grip on the center, which gives you time to push your b-pawn and create real threats.

I'd play Ngf6. Your bishop on c5 wandered into the middle of the board where it doesn't do much and can get chased, so instead let's develop with a purpose. Bringing the knight to f6 gets a piece off the back rank, guards your important center pawn on d5, and supports the knight on d7 at the same time — that's one move doing several useful jobs. After this you'll be ready to fianchetto your other bishop to g7, where it points powerfully across the board.

Takeaway: In the opening, develop pieces to squares where they defend your center and stay safe, not just to active-looking outposts.

I'd play b4. Your Bb3 is safe, but a bit passive: it only defends the pawn on d5, while b4 immediately challenges Black’s pawn on c5 and tries to create play on the queenside. If Black responds with cxb4, axb4 gives White a clearer pawn plan and more space to work with. This is the kind of active pawn break you should look for when your pieces are already stable.

I'd play Bg7. 

Your move, Bc5, develops a piece, but it leaves your kingside slightly disorganized since you already have a pawn on g6. By playing Bg7, you place your bishop on its ideal long diagonal, firmly defending your strong center pawns. This harmonizes your setup and gets you one step closer to castling your king to safety.

Takeaway: When you push a knight's pawn like g6, the most natural and powerful square for your bishop is right behind it.

I'd play b4. Your Bb3 was a reasonable idea — supporting the d5 pawn — but it's too passive when you have a chance to open the queenside where Black's pawns on c5 and b6 are a bit loose. Takeaway: When your opponent's pawns are fixed and slightly weak, prefer an active pawn break that opens lines over a passive defensive placement.

I'd play b4. 

While Bb3 is a reasonable prophylactic move to support your d5 pawn, it's a bit slow in a position where Black is already building a lot of space and pressure on the kingside. When your opponent is attacking on one wing or dominating the center, the best remedy is often to strike on the other side of the board. Playing b4 immediately challenges Black's queenside structure by attacking the c5 pawn, forcing them to make a decision and helping you open lines, like the a-file, for counterplay. 

Takeaway: When under pressure on one side of the board, look for pawn breaks to create active counterplay on the opposite wing.

I'd play Nc6. Your move tried to handle the position with a pawn, but Nc6 is cleaner: it develops a minor piece and also defends the pawn on e5 and the pawn on a7. The concrete plan is simple: improve your worst-placed piece first, then decide how to handle the queen and king situation.  
Takeaway: When a pawn move and a developing move both seem possible, prefer the developing move if it also solves a real problem.

I'd play Nc6. The problem with c6 is that it pushes the enemy knight away but leaves your own pieces sitting undeveloped, and it weakens your position without helping you get anything going. Nc6 does two useful jobs at once: it brings your last minor piece into the game and quietly defends your e5 pawn, so you're building a solid, connected setup while chasing the knight on d5 will come naturally afterward. Develop first, and let your pieces do the defending instead of your pawns.

Takeaway: When you're behind in development, bring a piece into play that also solves a problem, rather than making a pawn move that only pushes an enemy piece around.

I'd play Bxe6. This fixes the key mistake: instead of using the queen to take on e6 while she is loose, let the bishop take the rook and keep your queen active. A simple plan is Bxe6, and if ...Qc5, Qg8+ keeps the attack going.

I'd play Nc6. 

It's very tempting to play c6 to immediately kick that annoying knight on d5, but doing so creates dangerous weaknesses around your king and opens diagonals for White's queen. Instead, Nc6 is a calm, developing move that brings your last minor piece into the game while defending important central squares. Once your pieces are out, you'll be in a much better position to deal with White's centralized knight safely.

Takeaway: Focus on completing your development rather than making pawn moves to chase away active enemy pieces.

I'd play Bxe6. You had the right idea—win that rook on e6—but you grabbed it with the wrong piece: your queen was already attacked by the black queen on c3, so taking with the queen throws it away for free. Capturing with the bishop instead wins the rook while keeping your queen safely active on e8, and it even hits the pawn on f5 as a bonus. Before any capture, always ask "is the piece I'm moving safe where it lands?"—here the bishop lands safely and the queen stays out of trouble.

Takeaway: When two of your pieces can make the same capture, choose the one that isn't itself under attack.

I'd play Kb2. This keeps your king safer than Kb4 and makes it useful: it defends both the rook on b1 and the queen on c2. The concrete plan is simple: if Black plays Rxc2+, you can answer Rxc2 and keep things together.  
Takeaway: When your king must move, choose a square that also defends your important pieces.

I'd play Bxe6. 

Capturing the rook with your bishop is much stronger than grabbing it with the queen. By playing Bxe6, you win the rook while keeping your most powerful piece on the board to coordinate a dangerous attack against the black king. When you played Qxe6, you gave up your queen for a rook, losing your biggest offensive weapon and letting Black off the hook. 

Takeaway: When you have the choice of which piece to capture with, look for the option that keeps your most valuable attacking pieces on the board.

I'd play Kb2. Your king was in real danger on that side of the board, and Kb4 walked it out into the open where it becomes a target. Kb2 tucks the king safely behind your pieces while still guarding both the rook on b1 and the queen on c2, so nothing hangs. When you're under pressure, the priority is getting your king to safety and making sure all your pieces are protected before anything else.

Takeaway: When your king is exposed, step it toward shelter and defended squares, not out into the open.

I'd play Kb2. 

Your king was stuck in a dangerous pin on the c-file, with the black rook staring directly at it through your queen. While stepping away with the king was the right idea, walking up the board with Kb4 puts your king in even more danger. Playing Kb2 safely steps out of the pin while keeping your king tucked away and defending your rook, allowing you to deal with the incoming queen trade under better circumstances.

Takeaway: When breaking a pin by moving your king, step backward into safety rather than forward into the open board.

I'd play Rd3. This is a calm improving move: the rook steps up and defends both the c3-pawn and the d4-pawn. Your Bh6 move did make an attack on the rook on f8, but here the more reusable idea is to secure your own position first, then build from a stable base. If Black continues with ...Qa5, you have already strengthened the key pawns you need to hold your position together.

Takeaway: Before starting an attack, check whether one quiet move can make your own structure harder to challenge.

I'd play Rd3. Bishop to h6 looks aggressive, but it doesn't actually win the rook on f8 — after Black just moves it away, your bishop has left the strong central post and done nothing useful. The real issue in your position is that Black is ready to play a queen move to a5, hitting into your queenside where your king lives; Rd3 is a flexible, calm move that shores up your c3 and d4 pawns and gets the rook ready to swing over to defend the king's neighborhood. Before you go hunting for attacks, make sure your own side of the board is solid.

Takeaway: A threatening-looking move is only good if the threat is real — otherwise, reinforce your weakest area first.

I'd play Rd3. 

Your move, Bh6, goes for an immediate threat on the rook, but Black can easily step away and then launch a dangerous counterattack by bringing their queen to a5 to target your queenside pawns. By playing Rd3 first, you solidly defend your c3 pawn and prepare to meet Black's queenside threats. Once your king's position is secure, you can return to building your own active plans.

Takeaway: Before launching a one-move threat, check if your opponent has a dangerous counterattack brewing that you need to secure first.

I'd play b3. Instead of pushing with e6, which commits your advanced pawn too far, b3 calmly attacks the pawn on c4 and asks Black to justify their space. If Black answers with ...fxe5, then Nxe5 keeps your pieces active and your structure more flexible.  
Takeaway: When you’re tempted to force matters, first look for a simple pawn break that attacks the base of the opponent’s setup.

I'd play Bf2. The problem with e6 is that it shoves the pawn forward and hands Black a target — the pawn gets cut off and you lose the tension that was working for you. Instead, tuck your dark-squared bishop back to f2 to keep it safe on the long diagonal; then even if Black plays fxe5, you have Ng5 with active play against his kingside, and your center stays flexible rather than becoming a liability. The idea is to improve your worst-placed piece and keep the pressure without committing your pawns prematurely.

Takeaway: When your position is already pleasant, resist forcing pawn pushes — quietly improve a piece and keep the tension on your terms.

I'd play Re4. Your idea with Rd1 to attack the knight wasn't bad thinking, but that knight is well-defended and easily slips away, so you don't win anything. Instead, Re4 goes after the loose pawn on c4, and if Black defends with his knight, you simply capture it with Rxc4 and win a free pawn. Look for targets that can't easily be protected — that's where a rook does real work.

Takeaway: Attack the weakness you can actually win, not the piece that just runs away.

I'd play Re4. Your Rd1 was logical because it attacks the knight on d5 and defends the bishop on c1, but Re4 is more active and easier to build a plan around. The rook immediately attacks the pawn on c4, so you are not just reacting—you are giving Black a problem to solve. Takeaway: When you can, improve a piece with a direct target instead of only making a defensive move.

I'd play Bf2. 

Pushing your pawn to e6 releases the central tension too early and allows Black to set up a comfortable blockade. Instead, as an advanced player, it's often better to maintain that tension while preparing for the opponent's inevitable pawn breaks. By dropping the bishop back to f2, you proactively move it to a safer square so that when Black captures on e5, you are perfectly positioned to respond dynamically, such as by jumping your knight to g5. 

Takeaway: Maintain central tension and use prophylactic moves to ensure your pieces are ideally placed when that tension finally breaks.

I'd play Re4.

Your move, Rd1, is a logical attempt to put pressure on the knight in the center. However, attacking the weak pawn on c4 with your rook is even more effective. That pawn is currently undefended and is cramping your position on the queenside, so putting immediate pressure on it forces Black to make uncomfortable decisions and gives you a chance to win it for free.

Takeaway: Actively target your opponent's undefended, advanced pawns to win material and create space for your pieces.

I'd play Qc7. Grabbing on f6 felt natural, but it walked your queen into the fire and gave White dangerous activity — instead, notice that White's own pieces are loose. From c7 your queen simultaneously eyes the exposed rook on b6 and the knight on c6, so White has to scramble to untangle; after something like Rbb1 you can calmly reposition with Qd6 and keep your extra material and coordination. The key shift is defense-before-greed: that f6-pawn wasn't going anywhere, and the winning idea was to punish White's overextended pieces, not to snatch a pawn into an attack.

Takeaway: Before capturing, check whether your opponent's own pieces are hanging — often the strongest move is one that attacks two loose targets at once.

I'd play Qc7. Instead of grabbing the loose pawn right away, put the queen on a more active and safer square: it attacks the rook on b6 and the knight on c6 while also defending the pawn on f7. That gives Black a clear plan—create pressure on White’s advanced pieces before cashing in material.  
Takeaway: When a capture is available, first ask whether a forcing improving move creates bigger problems for your opponent.

I'd play Rxb2. Instead of giving a check with Rd8+, take the undefended pawn on b2 right away. This also attacks the pawn on a2 and defends your pawn on b4, so one move improves both your material and your structure. Takeaway: When there’s no urgent tactic, prefer the move that wins a loose pawn while improving your pieces.

I'd play h5. Your Rd8+ was tempting because a check feels forcing, but here it just chases the white king to a square it's happy to go to, and you don't actually gain anything—the check doesn't lead to a follow-up. Instead, notice that the bishop on g4 has no safe, active retreat once you hit it with h5: after the bishop moves, your rook on g2 gets to swing over to g3 with real activity, keeping pressure while you still eye the loose pawn on b2. This is the difference between a check that fizzles and a threat that improves your position.

Takeaway: Before giving a check, ask what it actually accomplishes—an unforcing move that gains a target or activity often beats an empty check.

I'd play Qc7. 

Capturing the f6 pawn with your queen looks tempting, but it walks right into a dangerous tactical minefield and leaves your king exposed. Instead, sliding the queen over to c7 is a highly resourceful defensive move. It steps away from the tension while actively counterattacking both White's rook on b6 and knight on c6, forcing White to scramble to defend their own pieces rather than launching an attack on yours.

Takeaway: When your opponent's pieces are aggressively posted, look for active retreats that sidestep their threats while creating immediate problems of your own.

I'd play h5. 

Giving a check with your rook on d8 looks tempting, but White can simply block with their knight, placing it on a strong central square. Instead, attacking the loose bishop with h5 is much more testing. It forces the bishop to make a decision, and if White tries to counterattack your rook, you can take advantage of your active pieces to create a powerful pin against their king.

Takeaway: Before giving a check, consider whether the way your opponent blocks will actually improve their position.

I'd play Nd2. It is a calm developing move that fixes the immediate problem: your pawn on c4 was undefended, and this move protects it instead of pushing it forward with c5. For a beginner, this is a great habit—before advancing a pawn, ask whether it is safer and more useful to defend your important pawn chain first.  
Takeaway: When a pawn is under pressure, look for a developing move that defends it before pushing it.

I'd play cxd5. The problem with c5 is that it pushed past the tension and let your pawn on c4 become a target that's no longer defended — pushing usually gives up options and locks the position in your opponent's favor. Instead, when you have two pawns eyeing each other, capturing is often the cleanest choice: cxd5 wins the pawn back cleanly and, after Black recaptures with exd5, you've opened lines and kept a healthy structure. This also removes the worry about your c-pawn hanging around undefended.

Takeaway: When two pawns are staring at each other, capturing to resolve the tension is usually safer than shoving your pawn past it.

I'd play cxd5. 

Pushing your pawn to c5 locks up the queenside and releases the tension you've built up against Black's center. By instead capturing with cxd5, you deal with the pressure on your c4 pawn directly by removing one of its attackers. After Black recaptures with their e-pawn, the center stays open, giving your pieces more room to breathe and develop naturally.

Takeaway: When a pawn is under pressure, capturing an opposing pawn often solves the problem while keeping the center active.

I'd play b4. This is a direct, useful move: it attacks the pawn on a3 and gives Black a clear queenside plan instead of making a risky pawn move like f6. If White ignores it, Black can follow with bxa3, winning that pawn and opening lines on the queenside.  
Takeaway: When you can improve your position with a clear attack on a pawn, prefer that over a loosening pawn move.

I'd play Nb6. The trouble with f6 is that it weakens your kingside squares and doesn't help develop your pieces or connect your rooks — your queenside is still tangled up, with the rook on a8 and bishop on c8 waiting to breathe. Nb6 fixes exactly that: the knight covers both the a8-rook and the c8-bishop, so next you can freely develop that bishop and think about castling or shifting your king to safety. It's a quiet consolidating move that untangles you instead of creating new holes.

Takeaway: When your position is cramped and your king is still in the center, prioritize developing and connecting your pieces over pushing pawns that create weaknesses.

I'd play b4.

When White's king is tucked away on the queenside, your pawns on that side are your best attacking weapons. Your move f6 unnecessarily weakened your own position and didn't challenge White's setup. By pushing b4, you immediately attack the a3 pawn and start ripping open lines around White's king to create dangerous counterplay. 

Takeaway: When the opponent's king is sheltering on the queenside, look for pawn breaks like b4 to pry open files and create an attack.

I'd play h5. Your move Re1 was safe enough, but it was purely defensive and let Black off the hook — with h5 you go on the attack by kicking the knight on g6, taking away one of Black's active pieces. When you already have your pieces developed and aimed at the center, look for ways to make threats rather than just shoring up your own position. After the knight is forced to move, you can keep building pressure with your well-placed pieces.

Takeaway: When your pieces are active, look for a move that makes a threat instead of one that only defends.

I'd play h5. Re1 is understandable because it defends things, but here it is a bit too passive. h5 gives your move a clear purpose: it attacks the knight on g6 and makes Black respond to you. If Black plays a6, you have still created pressure instead of only making a defensive move.

Takeaway: When you can, choose an active move that attacks a piece rather than a quiet move that only defends.

I'd play h5. 

Your move makes sense because it moves your rook away from the attack of Black's bishop, but it is a bit passive. I'd play d5. I'd play c4. Look for ways to build pressure on a single point like you did here, while keeping your own pieces safe. 

Takeaway: Put your knights where they attack important targets and stay out of danger.

I'd play Nf4. You’re right to want to attack White’s pawns, and moving your knight to f4 puts real pressure on the h3 pawn—this creates a clear threat that White has to respond to. Your earlier move, Nb2, tried to attack too many things at once, which made it harder to follow up. Nf4 is simpler: one target, one clear plan, and it keeps your knight active near the center where it can keep helping. Takeaway: Focus on one strong threat at a time—it’s more powerful than trying to do everything.

I'd play Qc2. By moving your queen back, you protect the vulnerable a2 pawn and support your rook on c1, while also threatening their bishop on c8. This keeps your queen safe and avoids leaving pieces undefended like your previous move did. Takeaway: Prioritize keeping your queen protected while developing pieces to active squares.

I'd play Nf4. This move is a good choice because it attacks the pawn on h3, which can help Black gain some counterplay and distract White from the knight on d3. By playing Nf4, you're also preparing to potentially move your king to a safer position, which is a good idea since your king is currently a bit exposed. Your move Nb2 wasn't bad, but it didn't address the issue of your knight being undefended, and Nf4 is a more active and flexible choice.
Takeaway: When your pieces are under attack, look for moves that not only save them but also create new possibilities to counter-attack.

I'd play Nf4. You spotted that the knight on d3 was under attack, and wanting to do something about it is good! However, Nb2 doesn't really *defend* the knight, and it takes the knight away from helping to attack White’s pieces. Nf4 is a much stronger move because it directly challenges White’s pawn on h3, and opens up possibilities to develop your attack – you could follow up by bringing your bishop into the game. 

Takeaway: Before moving a piece, double-check if it’s actually helping to defend something or actively creating a threat.

I'd play Nf4. The pawn on h3 is undefended, so with Nf4 you attack it and also step the knight closer to your king for safety. Your move, Nb2, threatened the bishop but allowed White an easy defense next turn — here you want to look for targets that can't be defended. Takeaway: Attack undefended pieces first before threatening ones your opponent can easily protect.

I'd play fxg7.

Your move Rxd1 traded your rook for a knight, which loses material unnecessarily when you had a much simpler capture available. Here, taking the pawn on g7 also attacks Black's rook on h8, so you're getting two things done at once—it's the kind of capture that gives your opponent immediate problems to solve. After this, you can look to capture the knight on d1 next, since it's also undefended and your bishop on g5 is already aimed that way.

Takeaway: Before trading pieces, check if you have a capture that creates an extra threat.

I'd play Nf4. You spotted that your knight is under attack, which is great—always pay attention when your pieces are threatened! But instead of running to the side with Nb2, look for a square where the knight does something useful right away. On f4, it eyes the h3 pawn and keeps pressure on White’s position, giving you time to regroup and maybe even win that pawn later. It’s a simple idea with clear benefits, so you’ll remember it for future games.

Takeaway: When a piece is attacked, ask: “Can I move it to a square where it still helps my game?”

I'd play Nf4. Your knight is already attacked by White's rook, and moving it to f4 solves that problem while also attacking the pawn on h3—doing two good things at once. Compare this to Nb2: while that move attacks two pieces, it doesn't pay attention to the immediate threat against your knight, which is the most urgent thing to address. Takeaway: When one of your pieces is under attack, your first priority is dealing with that threat—either defend the piece or move it somewhere safe.

I'd play Bc7. The student lost a tempo by recapturing with the pawn instead of using their bishop, which is a powerful piece here. Moving the bishop to c7 directly challenges White’s knight on d6, a critical attacker, and helps protect Black’s queen in a much more flexible way. Playing Bc7 keeps the position active and helps control key central squares.

Takeaway: Use your pieces to defend and create space — not just pawns.

I'd play Bc7. You took the pawn on d4 thinking you’d gain material, but that left your queen exposed and weakened your center—Bc7 does something better: it protects your queen while putting pressure on White’s knight sitting on d6, which is a big target. This move keeps your position solid and gives you a clear plan: look for chances to trade that knight or open lines on the c-file. You’re not just reacting—you’re building a quiet, powerful threat that White must respond to.  
Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong central piece, look for moves that attack it while protecting your own key pieces.

I'd play Qg5+.

You found the right idea—attacking with a check—but the queen does more work on the kingside. After Qg5+, your queen hits the d5 pawn and the g6 pawn while keeping an eye on Black's king, and you can follow up by bringing your rook into the game. Compare this to Qe7+: there the queen gets tangled near Black's rooks, while here she stays active and keeps your options open.

Takeaway: When choosing between checks, pick the one that leaves your pieces on flexible squares with multiple targets.

I'd play h6. Your instinct to challenge White’s pieces with cxd4 is good – you’re right to look for active moves! However, that pawn grab opens the position too early and lets White develop their pieces with a clear advantage. Instead, h6 immediately puts pressure on the bishop and forces White to react, preparing to trade it off for your knight and opening the h-file for potential attack. Takeaway: Before making a capture, always consider if it helps your opponent develop their pieces or improves their position.

I'd play fxg7. By capturing the pawn on g7, you attack Black's rook, forcing it to move—then your bishop can grab their queen! This turns your pawn push into a powerful double attack. The student's move gave up a strong pawn; here we defend by counterattacking. Takeaway: Look for captures that create threats to multiple pieces.

I'd play h6. This move is a great choice because it directly addresses the bishop on g5, which is putting pressure on your position, and it's a simple and concrete idea to understand and execute. Your mistake with cxd4 was likely trying to simplify the position, but it ended up losing some advantages, so it's better to focus on actively addressing the pieces that are putting pressure on you. Takeaway: When faced with an opponent's piece that's exerting influence, consider playing a move that directly challenges or addresses it.

I'd play Nf4. By attacking the h3 pawn, you create immediate pressure on White's weakened kingside. This forces them to defend with their rook or bishop, limiting their options. Your knight becomes active near their king, setting up potential future threats. Takeaway: Target undefended pawns to restrict your opponent's pieces.

I'd play h6.  
This simple move attacks the bishop on g5, which is currently putting pressure on your knight on e7. It’s a useful reminder to break pins before they become a bigger problem, and it gives your king some breathing room. Since your last move gave White a strong central pawn, focusing on completing your development and securing your position is key here.

I'd play h6. You grabbed space with cxd4, but that just helped White’s pawns march forward—notice how the d4 pawn now eyes c5 and e5. Instead, h6 kicks the bishop away before it can annoy you with checks; if it takes your knight, you recapture with the queen and suddenly your king feels safer and your minor pieces breathe. It’s a small move that solves a real problem: that bishop was pinching your knight like a thorn while White’s queen and knight loomed over your position.

Takeaway: When your opponent has a strong bishop on g5, ask yourself if a well-timed …h6 can shoo it away and unhook your pieces.

I'd play Nd5. This move takes advantage of the knight's flexibility by attacking the pawn on c4, creating a fork threat that can disrupt White’s central control. The student's h5 was weakening the kingside structure needlessly; in contrast, Nd5 focuses on improving the piece and increasing pressure near White's king. It keeps the initiative under control while maintaining the balance of forces.

Takeaway: Look for active knight moves that create threats, not just to gain tempo, but to guide your opponent’s attention to weak areas of their position.

I'd play h6. This move gets you moving again with a clear purpose: you challenge the bishop on g5 and force White to declare their intentions. If they capture on e7, you recapture with your queen and have solved your development troubles; if they retreat, you've gained space and can keep improving your position. Takeaway: When pieces feel tight, look for moves that force your opponent to respond and clarify the position.

I'd play Bc5. Your move h5 was aiming to create space, but it ignored the real issue: your pawn on a7 is loose and your knight on d4 is under pressure from White’s bishop on d1. By playing Bc5, you tie down the bishop to defending a7 and simultaneously protect your knight — you’re not just reacting, you’re organizing your pieces to hold the center and prepare to push ...f5 or ...Nf5 next. This move turns defense into planning, and it’s exactly the kind of quiet, thoughtful move strong players make when they see beyond the immediate.  
Takeaway: Always ask which of your own pieces are undefended before creating new targets.

I'd play Bc5. This move is a great choice because it defends the pawn on a7 and the knight on d4, which are both important for Black's position. By playing Bc5, you're also preparing to develop other pieces and put pressure on White's position, which is a key part of a solid strategy. Your move h5 didn't quite achieve this, as it didn't address the potential weaknesses on a7 and d4, so let's focus on building a stronger foundation with Bc5. I'd play Bc7. Your move h5 tries to create something on the kingside, but it doesn’t really threaten anything immediate and weakens your pawn structure unnecessarily. Instead, Bc7 puts pressure on the white pawn on h2, opening up possibilities for a kingside attack if White doesn’t respond carefully—perhaps followed by g5 to continue the pressure. Developing your pieces with a clear target in mind will always be more effective than making random pawn moves!

I'd play Bc7. This move directly targets White's weak pawn on h2, creating immediate pressure that can't be ignored. Instead of your move, which didn't address the central tension, Bc7 sets up a real threat and forces your opponent to deal with a concrete problem you've created. Takeaway: First, find moves that create direct threats to undeveloped, exposed enemy kingside pawns.

I'd play Nd5.

Your move h5 gave White a hook to open the kingside with g4, and you didn’t have a clear follow-up. Nd5 is simple and strong: you sidestep any tricks on b6, keep the bishop pair, and even threaten to win the pawn on b2 with Nxb6 next. The knight sits safely on d5, eyeing both wings, and keeps the position flexible.

Takeaway: When your opponent’s pawn structure looks inviting, first activate your pieces so you can meet any break with a concrete threat.

I'd play Qc4.

Your queen on c7 is beautifully placed, but grabbing the pawn on e5 was greedy—Black's knight there is defended, and your queen will get chased around while Black develops. Instead, Qc4 keeps your queen active and safe, attacking two bishops and the f7 pawn while also guarding your a2 pawn and the c1 rook. From here you can continue developing with a discovered threat in mind, or simply slide back to c2 next move to attack the c8 bishop with tempo. This teaches the habit of asking "Is my queen safe AND active?" before touching it.

Takeaway: Centralize your pieces with a threat, not just a capture—activity beats material if it keeps you in the game.

I'd play Bc7. Your move with the h-pawn was passive when you have a golden opportunity to create concrete problems for White—this bishop move attacks the h2 pawn and forces White to respond to a real threat. The key idea at this level is recognizing when you can shift from maneuvering to forcing play: Bc7 creates a tactical tension that White must address immediately, compared to h5 which does nothing to challenge your opponent. This move also keeps your pieces active and coordinated while planting the seeds for further pressure along the long diagonal. Takeaway: When you see a move that creates an immediate threat, prioritize it over quiet pawn moves that don't change the position's tension.

I'd play Ne1.

Your move Nb2 looks active because it attacks two pieces, but it lets White untangle with Bc2. At your level, it's easy to get excited about forking or hitting lots of things, but ask first: does my opponent even have to cooperate? Here, White just moves the bishop and your knight has to scramble again.

Ne1 creates a more useful purpose: it eyes the f3 pawn and prepares to harass the white rook with a check on e5. The plan is simple—improve your knight, then shake up White's coordination. This teaches the habit of asking "what's my piece's *job*?" rather than just counting attacked squares.

Takeaway: Before moving a piece to a flashy square, ask what your opponent's easiest reply is and whether your piece will still be useful after it.

I'd play h6. By pushing the pawn to h6, you attack White's bishop on g5, forcing it to either retreat or capture your knight—if it takes, you recapture with your queen, trading a knight for their active bishop. This addresses the immediate threat of their bishop pinning your knight and helps you regain control of the center. Takeaway: Challenge pinned pieces to disrupt your opponent's coordination.

I'd play Nd5. This centralizes your knight while securing the bishop on b6, maintaining your strong outpost on d4. The student's h5 created unnecessary kingside weaknesses instead of reinforcing key pieces – here, protecting the bishop allows you to keep pressure on White's position. Takeaway: Prioritize defending active pieces before making pawn moves that expose your structure.

I'd play h6.

You grabbed on d4, but that opens the c-file for White's rook and makes your bishop on b6 look shaky. Instead, h6 asks a direct question: where does that bishop go? After h6, White's only consistent move is Bxe7+, and after Qxe7 you've solved your opening problems smoothly—your king is safer, your pieces coordinate better, and you've avoided creating new weaknesses. This is the kind of patient, "ask first, capture later" thinking that separates intermediate players from those who snatch pawns and regret it.

Takeaway: When you have a chance to improve your position by forcing a piece trade, take it before committing to an irreversible capture that opens lines for your opponent.

I'd play Nd5.

Your h5 pushes a kingside pawn that doesn't help your coordination. Instead, Nd5 centralizes your knight with purpose: it defends your bishop on b6 and eyes squares around White's king. From d5, your knight becomes a flexible piece—ready to support ...e3 ideas or jump to c3 to stir up trouble—while keeping your structure intact. This is the kind of proactive central move that defines play at your level.

Takeaway: Centralize knights with a clear defensive purpose before committing to wing pawn pushes.
